question:
Given real numbers $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{2019}$ with $b_{2019} \neq 0$, let $z_1,z_2,\dots,z_{2019}$ be 
the roots in the complex plane of the polynomial 
\[
P(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{2019} b_k z^k.
\]
Let $\mu = (|z_1| + \cdots + |z_{2019}|)/2019$ be the average of the distances from $z_1,z_2,\dots,z_{2019}$ to the origin. Determine the largest constant $M$ such that $\mu \geq M$ for all choices of $b_0,b_1,\dots, b_{2019}$ that satisfy
\[
1 \leq b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots < b_{2019} \leq 2019.
\]
solution:
The answer is $M = 2019^{-1/2019}$. For any choices of $b_0,\ldots,b_{2019}$ as specified, AM-GM gives
\[
\mu \geq |z_1\cdots z_{2019}|^{1/2019} = |b_0/b_{2019}|^{1/2019} \geq 2019^{-1/2019}.
\]
To see that this is best possible, consider $b_0,\ldots,b_{2019}$ given by $b_k = 2019^{k/2019}$ for all $k$. Then 
\[
P(z/2019^{1/2019}) = \sum_{k=0}^{2019} z^k = \frac{z^{2020}-1}{z-1}
\]
has all of its roots on the unit circle. It follows that all of the roots of $P(z)$ have modulus $2019^{-1/2019}$, and so $\mu = 2019^{-1/2019}$ in this case.

descriptive_long.question:
Given real numbers $coefzero, coefone, \dots, coeflast$ with $coeflast \neq 0$, let $rootone,z_2,\dots,rootlast$ be 
the roots in the complex plane of the polynomial 
\[
polyfunc(complexvar) = \sum_{indexk=0}^{2019} coefkth\, complexvar^{indexk}.
\]
Let $avgdist = (|rootone| + \cdots + |rootlast|)/2019$ be the average of the distances from $rootone,z_2,\dots,rootlast$ to the origin. Determine the largest constant $maxconst$ such that $avgdist \geq maxconst$ for all choices of $coefzero,coefone,\dots, coeflast$ that satisfy
\[
1 \leq coefzero < coefone < b_2 < \cdots < coeflast \leq 2019.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
The answer is $maxconst = 2019^{-1/2019}$. For any choices of $coefzero,\ldots,coeflast$ as specified, AM-GM gives
\[
avgdist \geq |rootone\cdots rootlast|^{1/2019} = |coefzero/coeflast|^{1/2019} \geq 2019^{-1/2019}.
\]
To see that this is best possible, consider $coefzero,\ldots,coeflast$ given by $coefkth = 2019^{indexk/2019}$ for all $indexk$. Then 
\[
polyfunc\!\bigl(complexvar/2019^{1/2019}\bigr) = \sum_{indexk=0}^{2019} complexvar^{indexk} = \frac{complexvar^{2020}-1}{complexvar-1}
\]
has all of its roots on the unit circle. It follows that all of the roots of $polyfunc(complexvar)$ have modulus $2019^{-1/2019}$, and so $avgdist = 2019^{-1/2019}$ in this case.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Given real numbers $dustymelon, ivorybeach, \dots, silverquill$ with $silverquill \neq 0$, let $papercrane,z_2,\dots,coppervase$ be the roots in the complex plane of the polynomial
\[
clocktower(snowglobe) = \sum_{lacemarker=0}^{2019} velvetbloom\, snowglobe^{lacemarker}.
\]
Let $oceanfever = (|papercrane| + \cdots + |coppervase|)/2019$ be the average of the distances from $papercrane,z_2,\dots,coppervase$ to the origin. Determine the largest constant riverstone such that $oceanfever \geq riverstone$ for all choices of $dustymelon,ivorybeach,\dots, silverquill$ that satisfy
\[
1 \leq dustymelon < ivorybeach < b_2 < \cdots < silverquill \leq 2019.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The answer is $riverstone = 2019^{-1/2019}$. For any choices of $dustymelon,\ldots,silverquill$ as specified, AM-GM gives
\[
oceanfever \geq |papercrane\cdots coppervase|^{1/2019} = |dustymelon/silverquill|^{1/2019} \geq 2019^{-1/2019}.
\]
To see that this is best possible, consider $dustymelon,\ldots,silverquill$ given by $velvetbloom = 2019^{lacemarker/2019}$ for all $lacemarker$. Then
\[
clocktower\bigl(snowglobe/2019^{1/2019}\bigr) = \sum_{lacemarker=0}^{2019} snowglobe^{lacemarker} = \frac{snowglobe^{2020}-1}{snowglobe-1}
\]
has all of its roots on the unit circle. It follows that all of the roots of $clocktower(snowglobe)$ have modulus $2019^{-1/2019}$, and so $oceanfever = 2019^{-1/2019}$ in this case.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Given real numbers $lastcoefficient, latecoefficient, \dots, minorcoefficient$ with $minorcoefficient \neq 0$, let $realrootone,z_2,\dots,realrootmax$ be 
the roots in the complex plane of the polynomial 
\[
constantpoly(realitynumber) = \sum_{invariable=0}^{2019} unfixedcoefficient realitynumber^{invariable}.
\]
Let $extremevalue = (|realrootone| + \cdots + |realrootmax|)/2019$ be the average of the distances from $realrootone,z_2,\dots,realrootmax$ to the origin. Determine the largest constant $tinyconstant$ such that $extremevalue \geq tinyconstant$ for all choices of $lastcoefficient,latecoefficient,\dots, minorcoefficient$ that satisfy
\[
1 \leq lastcoefficient < latecoefficient < b_2 < \cdots < minorcoefficient \leq 2019.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The answer is $tinyconstant = 2019^{-1/2019}$. For any choices of $lastcoefficient,\ldots,minorcoefficient$ as specified, AM-GM gives
\[
extremevalue \geq |realrootone\cdots realrootmax|^{1/2019} = |lastcoefficient/minorcoefficient|^{1/2019} \geq 2019^{-1/2019}.
\]
To see that this is best possible, consider $lastcoefficient,\ldots,minorcoefficient$ given by $unfixedcoefficient = 2019^{invariable/2019}$ for all $invariable$. Then 
\[
constantpoly\left(realitynumber/2019^{1/2019}\right) = \sum_{invariable=0}^{2019} realitynumber^{invariable} = \frac{realitynumber^{2020}-1}{realitynumber-1}
\]
has all of its roots on the unit circle. It follows that all of the roots of $constantpoly(realitynumber)$ have modulus $2019^{-1/2019}$, and so $extremevalue = 2019^{-1/2019}$ in this case.

garbled_string.question:
Given real numbers $qcivgnlm, rpkhqzad, \dots, tmlghowe$ with $tmlghowe \neq 0$, let $mcdhsgal,z_2,\dots,yajvfncq$ be 
the roots in the complex plane of the polynomial 
\[
qzxwvtnp(pxoriflm) = \sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{2019} vnusbqea pxoriflm^{hjgrksla}.
\]
Let $tewqbzhn = (|mcdhsgal| + \cdots + |yajvfncq|)/2019$ be the average of the distances from $mcdhsgal,z_2,\dots,yajvfncq$ to the origin. Determine the largest constant $pfjsoerw$ such that $tewqbzhn \geq pfjsoerw$ for all choices of $qcivgnlm,rpkhqzad,\dots, tmlghowe$ that satisfy
\[
1 \leq qcivgnlm < rpkhqzad < b_2 < \cdots < tmlghowe \leq 2019.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
The answer is $pfjsoerw = 2019^{-1/2019}$. For any choices of $qcivgnlm,\ldots,tmlghowe$ as specified, AM-GM gives
\[
tewqbzhn \geq |mcdhsgal\cdots yajvfncq|^{1/2019} = |qcivgnlm/tmlghowe|^{1/2019} \geq 2019^{-1/2019}.
\]
To see that this is best possible, consider $qcivgnlm,\ldots,tmlghowe$ given by $vnusbqea = 2019^{hjgrksla/2019}$ for all $hjgrksla$. Then 
\[
qzxwvtnp(pxoriflm/2019^{1/2019}) = \sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{2019} pxoriflm^{hjgrksla} = \frac{pxoriflm^{2020}-1}{pxoriflm-1}
\]
has all of its roots on the unit circle. It follows that all of the roots of $qzxwvtnp(pxoriflm)$ have modulus $2019^{-1/2019}$, and so $tewqbzhn = 2019^{-1/2019}$ in this case.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $N\ge 2$ and a real parameter $\beta>0$.  
Let  
\[
Q(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{N}c_k z^{k},\qquad c_N\ne 0 ,
\]
be a complex polynomial whose \emph{real, positive} coefficients satisfy  

(I) (upper-lower strip) $3\le c_0\le c_1\le\cdots\le c_N\le 3N$;  

(II) (log-convexity)  
\[
\frac{c_{k+1}}{c_k}\le\frac{c_{k+2}}{c_{k+1}},\qquad 0\le k\le N-2;
\]

(III) (prescribed logarithmic mean)  
\[
\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}\ln c_k=\Lambda,\qquad 
\ln(3\sqrt N)\le \Lambda\le \ln(3N).
\]

Denote the (complex) roots of $Q$ by $w_1,\dots ,w_N$ and introduce the
order-$\beta$ power mean of their moduli
\[
\mu_\beta=\Bigl(\tfrac{|w_1|^\beta+\dots+|w_N|^\beta}{N}\Bigr)^{1/\beta},
\qquad \beta>0.
\]

(a)  Determine, in closed form, the largest constant
\[
M_{N,\beta}=M_{N,\beta}(3,3N,\Lambda)
\]
such that
\[
\mu_\beta\ge M_{N,\beta}
\]
for \emph{every} polynomial whose coefficients obey
\textup{(I)}, \textup{(II)} and \textup{(III)}.

(b)  For all admissible triples $(N,\beta,\Lambda)$ describe, \emph{up
to permutations of the roots}, all coefficient families for which the
equality $\mu_\beta=M_{N,\beta}$ is attained.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
r_j:=|w_j|,\qquad
G:=(r_1r_2\cdots r_N)^{1/N}.
\]

\textbf{1.  Geometric mean of the root moduli}  
Factoring $Q$ yields
\[
Q(z)=c_N\prod_{j=1}^{N}(z-w_j),\qquad 
(-1)^N \frac{c_0}{c_N}=w_1\cdots w_N.
\]
Hence
\[
G=\Bigl|\frac{c_0}{c_N}\Bigr|^{1/N}.
\tag{1}
\]

\textbf{2.  Power-mean inequality}  
For every $\beta>0$ and every positive real numbers
$r_1,\dots ,r_N$,
\[
\mu_\beta\ge G
\tag{2}
\]
(the classical power-mean hierarchy).  Consequently the universal
constant $M_{N,\beta}$ is determined by the \emph{minimum} of the ratio
$c_0/c_N$ under (I)-(III).

\textbf{3.  Logarithmic variables}  
Put $x_k:=\ln c_k\;(0\le k\le N)$ and denote forward differences by
$d_k:=x_{k+1}-x_k$.  Then  

(a) $\ln 3\le x_0\le x_1\le\cdots\le x_N\le\ln(3N)$;

(b) $0\le d_0\le d_1\le\cdots\le d_{N-1}$ (discrete convexity);

(c) $\tfrac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k=\Lambda$.

We must minimise
\[
\Phi(x_0,\dots ,x_N):=x_0-x_N
\tag{3}
\]
subject to (a)-(c).

\textbf{4.  A discrete convexity lemma}

Lemma 1.  
For every discrete convex sequence
$(x_k)_{k=0}^{N}$ one has
\[
x_0\;\le\;\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k
\;\le\;\frac{x_0+x_N}{2}.
\tag{4}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Set $s_k:=x_k-x_0\;(0\le k\le N)$.  Then $s_0=0$, $s_k\ge 0$ and the
differences $s_{k+1}-s_k=d_k$ form a non-decreasing sequence.  Hence
$s_k\le\frac{k}{N}\,s_N$ for all $k$, so
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{N}s_k\le\sum_{k=0}^{N}\frac{k}{N}s_N
=\frac{N}{2}s_N.
\]
Dividing by $N+1$ and restoring the $x$-notation gives the right-hand
side of (4).  The left-hand side is obvious.  $\square$

\textbf{5.  Extremal sequences are affine}

Lemma 2 (rigidity).  
If an admissible sequence attains equality
$\tfrac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k=\tfrac{1}{2}(x_0+x_N)$ in (4), then
\[
x_k=x_0+\frac{k}{N}(x_N-x_0),\qquad 0\le k\le N.
\tag{5}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Define $y_k:=x_0+\frac{k}{N}(x_N-x_0)$.  The discrete convexity of
$(x_k)$ implies $x_k\le y_k$ for $0\le k\le N$, while the two sequences
share the same endpoints and the same arithmetic mean.  Hence their
termwise difference is a non-negative sequence with vanishing sum,
forcing $x_k=y_k$ for every $k$.  $\square$

\textbf{6.  Optimisation of the endpoints}  
Lemma 1 implies
\[
x_0+x_N\ge 2\Lambda\ge 2\ln(3\sqrt N)=\ln(9N).
\tag{6}
\]
Put $S:=x_0+x_N$ and $D:=x_N-x_0$.  Conditions (a) are rewritten as
\[
2\ln 3\le S-D,\quad S+D\le 2\ln(3N).
\tag{7}
\]
For fixed $S$ the maximal admissible $D$ equals
\[
D_{\max}(S)=\min\bigl\{\,S-2\ln 3,\;2\ln(3N)-S\,\bigr\}.
\]
The two arguments of the minimum are increasing and decreasing in $S$,
respectively, and they are equal at
\[
S_0:=\ln(9N).
\]
Because (6) shows $S\ge 2\Lambda\ge S_0$, the feasible interval for $S$
is $[\,2\Lambda,\,2\ln(3N)\,]$, entirely to the \emph{right} of $S_0$.
Hence $D_{\max}(S)$ is decreasing in that interval and attains its
maximum at $S=2\Lambda$.  Therefore
\[
D_{\max}=2\bigl(\ln(3N)-\Lambda\bigr).
\tag{8}
\]

\textbf{7.  The minimal coefficient ratio}  
Since $\Phi=-D$, we obtain from (8)
\[
\Bigl(\frac{c_0}{c_N}\Bigr)_{\min}=
\exp\!\bigl(-D_{\max}\bigr)=
\exp\!\bigl(2\Lambda-2\ln(3N)\bigr).
\tag{9}
\]
Substituting (9) in (2) and (1) gives the desired bound
\[
\boxed{%
M_{N,\beta}(3,3N,\Lambda)=
\exp\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2}{N}\bigl(\Lambda-\ln(3N)\bigr)\Bigr)}.
\tag{10}
\]
(The value is independent of $\beta$, although $\beta>0$ is required to
define $\mu_\beta$.)

\textbf{8.  Sharpness and equality}  

\emph{Equal root moduli.}  
Equality in (2) forces
$r_1=\dots=r_N=G$, i.e.\ all roots have the same modulus.

\emph{Affine logarithmic sequence.}  
Equality in (4) entails, by Lemma 2, that
$x_k$ is affine as in (5).  Consequently
\[
\frac{c_{k+1}}{c_k}=\rho\quad(0\le k\le N-1),
\qquad 
\rho:=\exp\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2}{N}\bigl(\ln(3N)-\Lambda\bigr)\Bigr)
\ge 1.
\tag{11}
\]
(The boundary value $\rho=1$ occurs exactly when
$\Lambda=\ln(3N)$.)

\emph{Determination of the scale factor.}  
Write $c_k=\gamma\,\rho^{\,k}$ $(0\le k\le N)$.  Condition (III) forces
\[
\Lambda=\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}\ln c_k
        =\ln\gamma+\frac{N}{2}\ln\rho
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\;
\gamma=\exp\!\bigl(2\Lambda-\ln(3N)\bigr)
\;}
\tag{12}
\]
so the scale factor is \emph{unique}.  Note that $\gamma$ automatically
satisfies $3\le \gamma\le 3N$ thanks to the bounds on $\Lambda$.

\emph{Root modulus.}  
Equations (1), (9) and (11) give
\[
r_1=\dots=r_N=\rho^{-1}=M_{N,\beta}.
\tag{13}
\]

\emph{Explicit equality polynomials.}  
Because all roots have modulus $\rho^{-1}$ and their product equals
$(-1)^N c_0/c_N=-\rho^{-N}$, they must be the $N$ points
$\rho^{-1}\zeta$ where $\zeta$ ranges over the $(N+1)$st roots of unity
different from $1$.  Hence
\[
Q(z)=\gamma\sum_{k=0}^{N}\rho^{\,k}z^{k}
     =\gamma\,\frac{1-(\rho z)^{N+1}}{1-\rho z}.
\tag{14}
\]
For $\Lambda=\ln(3N)$ one has $\rho=1$, $\gamma=3N$ and (14) reduces to
$Q(z)=3N\bigl(1+z+\dots +z^{N}\bigr)$, whose roots indeed lie on the
unit circle; the bound (10) becomes $M_{N,\beta}=1$.

\emph{Uniqueness.}  
Conversely, suppose $\mu_\beta=M_{N,\beta}$.  Then equalities occur in
both (2) and Lemma 1, whence $(x_k)$ is affine and the coefficients
must be the geometric progression (11) with \emph{the unique} initial
term (12).  Thus the only admissible coefficient family (up to
permutation of the roots, which does not change the polynomial) is
(14).

\textbf{9.  Answer to (a)-(b)}  

(a)  The largest constant is given by (10).

(b)  Equality holds precisely for the polynomial (14), whose coefficients
form the geometric progression $c_k=\gamma\rho^{\,k}$ with
$\rho$ from (11) and $\gamma$ from (12); the $N$ roots are
$\rho^{-1}\zeta$, where $\zeta$ runs through the $(N+1)$st roots of
unity distinct from $1$.
No other admissible polynomial attains the bound.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original setting, the enhanced variant is harder in
several independent directions.

1.  Additional structural constraints  
    • The coefficients are required to be **log-convex** (condition II),
      introducing majorisation/convexity considerations absent from the
      original problem.  
    • A **logarithmic mean** of the coefficients is prescribed
      (condition III), so the optimisation problem is now
      constrained by a *global* nonlinear condition instead of mere
      endpoint bounds.

2.  Variable parameter  
    The root‐mean to be bounded is the
    $\beta$–power mean, so the solver has to work simultaneously for a
    continuum of exponents.  Establishing a bound that is uniform in
    $\beta$ forces the use of power–mean theory rather than the single
    $\beta=1$ case of the kernel problem.

3.  Deeper optimisation theory  
    To identify the extremal sequence of coefficients one must invoke
    Karamata’s theorem, the Hardy–Littlewood–Pólya majorisation
    principle, or a full Lagrange–multiplier analysis on an
    $(N+1)$–dimensional simplex subject to convex ordering (II) and the
    nonlinear moment constraint (III).  None of these tools is touched
    in the original problem, which relies only on a single application
    of $\mathrm{AM}\ge\mathrm{GM}$.

4.  Verifying attainability  
    Showing that the geometric progression really satisfies all three
    coefficient conditions and yields equality for every $\beta$
    requires a careful scaling argument, followed by an explicit
    localisation of the roots via a Möbius transformation—again a layer
    of analysis that is unnecessary for the kernel exercise.

All these ingredients oblige the solver to weave together root‐product
formulas, power–mean inequalities, majorisation theory and explicit
factorisation—making the enhanced variant substantially more technical
and conceptually deeper than both the original problem and the current
kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $N\ge 2$ and a real parameter $\beta>0$.  
Let  
\[
Q(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{N}c_k z^{k},\qquad c_N\ne 0 ,
\]
be a complex polynomial whose \emph{real, positive} coefficients satisfy  

(I) (upper-lower strip) $3\le c_0\le c_1\le\cdots\le c_N\le 3N$;  

(II) (log-convexity)  
\[
\frac{c_{k+1}}{c_k}\le\frac{c_{k+2}}{c_{k+1}},\qquad 0\le k\le N-2;
\]

(III) (prescribed logarithmic mean)  
\[
\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}\ln c_k=\Lambda,\qquad 
\ln(3\sqrt N)\le \Lambda\le \ln(3N).
\]

Denote the (complex) roots of $Q$ by $w_1,\dots ,w_N$ and introduce the
order-$\beta$ power mean of their moduli
\[
\mu_\beta=\Bigl(\tfrac{|w_1|^\beta+\dots+|w_N|^\beta}{N}\Bigr)^{1/\beta},
\qquad \beta>0.
\]

(a)  Determine, in closed form, the largest constant
\[
M_{N,\beta}=M_{N,\beta}(3,3N,\Lambda)
\]
such that
\[
\mu_\beta\ge M_{N,\beta}
\]
for \emph{every} polynomial whose coefficients obey
\textup{(I)}, \textup{(II)} and \textup{(III)}.

(b)  For all admissible triples $(N,\beta,\Lambda)$ describe, \emph{up
to permutations of the roots}, all coefficient families for which the
equality $\mu_\beta=M_{N,\beta}$ is attained.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write
\[
r_j:=|w_j|,\qquad
G:=(r_1r_2\cdots r_N)^{1/N}.
\]

\textbf{1.  Geometric mean of the root moduli}  
Factoring $Q$ yields
\[
Q(z)=c_N\prod_{j=1}^{N}(z-w_j),\qquad 
(-1)^N \frac{c_0}{c_N}=w_1\cdots w_N.
\]
Hence
\[
G=\Bigl|\frac{c_0}{c_N}\Bigr|^{1/N}.
\tag{1}
\]

\textbf{2.  Power-mean inequality}  
For every $\beta>0$ and every positive real numbers
$r_1,\dots ,r_N$,
\[
\mu_\beta\ge G
\tag{2}
\]
(the classical power-mean hierarchy).  Consequently the universal
constant $M_{N,\beta}$ is determined by the \emph{minimum} of the ratio
$c_0/c_N$ under (I)-(III).

\textbf{3.  Logarithmic variables}  
Put $x_k:=\ln c_k\;(0\le k\le N)$ and denote forward differences by
$d_k:=x_{k+1}-x_k$.  Then  

(a) $\ln 3\le x_0\le x_1\le\cdots\le x_N\le\ln(3N)$;

(b) $0\le d_0\le d_1\le\cdots\le d_{N-1}$ (discrete convexity);

(c) $\tfrac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k=\Lambda$.

We must minimise
\[
\Phi(x_0,\dots ,x_N):=x_0-x_N
\tag{3}
\]
subject to (a)-(c).

\textbf{4.  A discrete convexity lemma}

Lemma 1.  
For every discrete convex sequence
$(x_k)_{k=0}^{N}$ one has
\[
x_0\;\le\;\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k
\;\le\;\frac{x_0+x_N}{2}.
\tag{4}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Set $s_k:=x_k-x_0\;(0\le k\le N)$.  Then $s_0=0$, $s_k\ge 0$ and the
differences $s_{k+1}-s_k=d_k$ form a non-decreasing sequence.  Hence
$s_k\le\frac{k}{N}\,s_N$ for all $k$, so
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{N}s_k\le\sum_{k=0}^{N}\frac{k}{N}s_N
=\frac{N}{2}s_N.
\]
Dividing by $N+1$ and restoring the $x$-notation gives the right-hand
side of (4).  The left-hand side is obvious.  $\square$

\textbf{5.  Extremal sequences are affine}

Lemma 2 (rigidity).  
If an admissible sequence attains equality
$\tfrac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}x_k=\tfrac{1}{2}(x_0+x_N)$ in (4), then
\[
x_k=x_0+\frac{k}{N}(x_N-x_0),\qquad 0\le k\le N.
\tag{5}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Define $y_k:=x_0+\frac{k}{N}(x_N-x_0)$.  The discrete convexity of
$(x_k)$ implies $x_k\le y_k$ for $0\le k\le N$, while the two sequences
share the same endpoints and the same arithmetic mean.  Hence their
termwise difference is a non-negative sequence with vanishing sum,
forcing $x_k=y_k$ for every $k$.  $\square$

\textbf{6.  Optimisation of the endpoints}  
Lemma 1 implies
\[
x_0+x_N\ge 2\Lambda\ge 2\ln(3\sqrt N)=\ln(9N).
\tag{6}
\]
Put $S:=x_0+x_N$ and $D:=x_N-x_0$.  Conditions (a) are rewritten as
\[
2\ln 3\le S-D,\quad S+D\le 2\ln(3N).
\tag{7}
\]
For fixed $S$ the maximal admissible $D$ equals
\[
D_{\max}(S)=\min\bigl\{\,S-2\ln 3,\;2\ln(3N)-S\,\bigr\}.
\]
The two arguments of the minimum are increasing and decreasing in $S$,
respectively, and they are equal at
\[
S_0:=\ln(9N).
\]
Because (6) shows $S\ge 2\Lambda\ge S_0$, the feasible interval for $S$
is $[\,2\Lambda,\,2\ln(3N)\,]$, entirely to the \emph{right} of $S_0$.
Hence $D_{\max}(S)$ is decreasing in that interval and attains its
maximum at $S=2\Lambda$.  Therefore
\[
D_{\max}=2\bigl(\ln(3N)-\Lambda\bigr).
\tag{8}
\]

\textbf{7.  The minimal coefficient ratio}  
Since $\Phi=-D$, we obtain from (8)
\[
\Bigl(\frac{c_0}{c_N}\Bigr)_{\min}=
\exp\!\bigl(-D_{\max}\bigr)=
\exp\!\bigl(2\Lambda-2\ln(3N)\bigr).
\tag{9}
\]
Substituting (9) in (2) and (1) gives the desired bound
\[
\boxed{%
M_{N,\beta}(3,3N,\Lambda)=
\exp\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2}{N}\bigl(\Lambda-\ln(3N)\bigr)\Bigr)}.
\tag{10}
\]
(The value is independent of $\beta$, although $\beta>0$ is required to
define $\mu_\beta$.)

\textbf{8.  Sharpness and equality}  

\emph{Equal root moduli.}  
Equality in (2) forces
$r_1=\dots=r_N=G$, i.e.\ all roots have the same modulus.

\emph{Affine logarithmic sequence.}  
Equality in (4) entails, by Lemma 2, that
$x_k$ is affine as in (5).  Consequently
\[
\frac{c_{k+1}}{c_k}=\rho\quad(0\le k\le N-1),
\qquad 
\rho:=\exp\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2}{N}\bigl(\ln(3N)-\Lambda\bigr)\Bigr)
\ge 1.
\tag{11}
\]
(The boundary value $\rho=1$ occurs exactly when
$\Lambda=\ln(3N)$.)

\emph{Determination of the scale factor.}  
Write $c_k=\gamma\,\rho^{\,k}$ $(0\le k\le N)$.  Condition (III) forces
\[
\Lambda=\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}\ln c_k
        =\ln\gamma+\frac{N}{2}\ln\rho
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\;
\gamma=\exp\!\bigl(2\Lambda-\ln(3N)\bigr)
\;}
\tag{12}
\]
so the scale factor is \emph{unique}.  Note that $\gamma$ automatically
satisfies $3\le \gamma\le 3N$ thanks to the bounds on $\Lambda$.

\emph{Root modulus.}  
Equations (1), (9) and (11) give
\[
r_1=\dots=r_N=\rho^{-1}=M_{N,\beta}.
\tag{13}
\]

\emph{Explicit equality polynomials.}  
Because all roots have modulus $\rho^{-1}$ and their product equals
$(-1)^N c_0/c_N=-\rho^{-N}$, they must be the $N$ points
$\rho^{-1}\zeta$ where $\zeta$ ranges over the $(N+1)$st roots of unity
different from $1$.  Hence
\[
Q(z)=\gamma\sum_{k=0}^{N}\rho^{\,k}z^{k}
     =\gamma\,\frac{1-(\rho z)^{N+1}}{1-\rho z}.
\tag{14}
\]
For $\Lambda=\ln(3N)$ one has $\rho=1$, $\gamma=3N$ and (14) reduces to
$Q(z)=3N\bigl(1+z+\dots +z^{N}\bigr)$, whose roots indeed lie on the
unit circle; the bound (10) becomes $M_{N,\beta}=1$.

\emph{Uniqueness.}  
Conversely, suppose $\mu_\beta=M_{N,\beta}$.  Then equalities occur in
both (2) and Lemma 1, whence $(x_k)$ is affine and the coefficients
must be the geometric progression (11) with \emph{the unique} initial
term (12).  Thus the only admissible coefficient family (up to
permutation of the roots, which does not change the polynomial) is
(14).

\textbf{9.  Answer to (a)-(b)}  

(a)  The largest constant is given by (10).

(b)  Equality holds precisely for the polynomial (14), whose coefficients
form the geometric progression $c_k=\gamma\rho^{\,k}$ with
$\rho$ from (11) and $\gamma$ from (12); the $N$ roots are
$\rho^{-1}\zeta$, where $\zeta$ runs through the $(N+1)$st roots of
unity distinct from $1$.
No other admissible polynomial attains the bound.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original setting, the enhanced variant is harder in
several independent directions.

1.  Additional structural constraints  
    • The coefficients are required to be **log-convex** (condition II),
      introducing majorisation/convexity considerations absent from the
      original problem.  
    • A **logarithmic mean** of the coefficients is prescribed
      (condition III), so the optimisation problem is now
      constrained by a *global* nonlinear condition instead of mere
      endpoint bounds.

2.  Variable parameter  
    The root‐mean to be bounded is the
    $\beta$–power mean, so the solver has to work simultaneously for a
    continuum of exponents.  Establishing a bound that is uniform in
    $\beta$ forces the use of power–mean theory rather than the single
    $\beta=1$ case of the kernel problem.

3.  Deeper optimisation theory  
    To identify the extremal sequence of coefficients one must invoke
    Karamata’s theorem, the Hardy–Littlewood–Pólya majorisation
    principle, or a full Lagrange–multiplier analysis on an
    $(N+1)$–dimensional simplex subject to convex ordering (II) and the
    nonlinear moment constraint (III).  None of these tools is touched
    in the original problem, which relies only on a single application
    of $\mathrm{AM}\ge\mathrm{GM}$.

4.  Verifying attainability  
    Showing that the geometric progression really satisfies all three
    coefficient conditions and yields equality for every $\beta$
    requires a careful scaling argument, followed by an explicit
    localisation of the roots via a Möbius transformation—again a layer
    of analysis that is unnecessary for the kernel exercise.

All these ingredients oblige the solver to weave together root‐product
formulas, power–mean inequalities, majorisation theory and explicit
factorisation—making the enhanced variant substantially more technical
and conceptually deeper than both the original problem and the current
kernel variant.
